content.decision_question: Is admissions-process reform, absent structural change to track entry points, capable of meaningfully reducing socioeconomic segregation, or does it primarily leave the segregation mechanism intact while offering political cover?
content.evidence_boundary: Further evidence (e.g., piloting admissions reforms and measuring changes in applicant SES composition and self-exclusion rates) could clarify the actual causal contribution of procedural versus behavioral drivers of segregation. It cannot determine whether a partial, lower-conflict reform is an acceptable trade-off against a more complete but riskier structural fix, since that involves weighing incommensurable goals of thoroughness and political viability.
content.tension: Reforming admissions procedures preserves the existing track structure and is politically framed as the primary lever under consideration, but evidence suggests self-exclusion and segregation are driven by family strategic behavior and structural funnel effects that procedural reform alone may not reach, raising the question of whether targeting process over structure is a genuine solution or a symbolic substitute.
content.title: Procedural fairness versus structural change as the locus of reform
value_poles: working within existing institutional and political constraints
